The **Kalmar Union**[[a]](#cite_note-1) was a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") in [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), agreed at [Kalmar](/wiki/Kalmar "Kalmar") in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") as designed by Queen [Margaret](/wiki/Margaret_I_of_Denmark "Margaret I of Denmark") of Denmark. From 1397 to 1523,[[1]](#cite_note-2) it joined under a single monarch the three kingdoms of [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") (then including much of present-day [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland")), and [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), together with [Norway's maritime colonies](/wiki/List_of_possessions_of_Norway#Former_dependencies_and_homelands "List of possessions of Norway")[[b]](#cite_note-3) (then including [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland"),[[c]](#cite_note-4) the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands"), and the [Northern Isles](/wiki/Northern_Isles "Northern Isles") of [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") and [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland")).

The union was not quite continuous; there were several short interruptions. Legally, the countries remained separate [sovereign states](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state"), but their domestic and foreign policies were directed by a common monarch. [Gustav Vasa](/wiki/Gustav_Vasa "Gustav Vasa")'s election as King of Sweden on 6 June 1523, and his triumphant [entry into Stockholm](/wiki/Conquest_of_Stockholm "Conquest of Stockholm") 11 days later, marked Sweden's final secession from the Kalmar Union.[[2]](#cite_note-5) The Danish king formally renounced his claim to Sweden in 1524 at the [Treaty of Malmö](/wiki/Treaty_of_Malm%C3%B6 "Treaty of Malmö").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Union_de_Kalmar.png/250px-Union_de_Kalmar.png)](/wiki/File:Union_de_Kalmar.png)

The Kalmar Union. Denmark, Sweden, Norway. But also Finland, Iceland, the Åland Islands, the Faroe Islands, Greenland, Svalbard, Jan Mayen, Orkney and Shetland. Along with Schleswig, Holstein, Lauenburg, Oldenburg, Pomerania, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania and Konigsberg

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kalmar_Union&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Inception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kalmar_Union&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Inception")]

The union was the work of Scandinavian aristocracy who sought to counter the influence of the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League"), a northern German trade league centered around the Baltic and North Seas. Denmark in particular was in a [power struggle with the League](/wiki/Danish-Hanseatic_Rivalry "Danish-Hanseatic Rivalry") and had recently suffered a humiliating defeat in the [Danish-Hanseatic War (1361-1370)](/wiki/Danish-Hanseatic_War_(1361-1370) "Danish-Hanseatic War (1361-1370)") that allowed the League to become even more powerful. On the personal level, the union was achieved by Queen [Margaret I of Denmark](/wiki/Margaret_I_of_Denmark "Margaret I of Denmark") (1353–1412). She was a daughter of King [Valdemar IV of Denmark](/wiki/Valdemar_IV_of_Denmark "Valdemar IV of Denmark") and had married King [Haakon VI of Norway](/wiki/Haakon_VI_of_Norway "Haakon VI of Norway") and Sweden, who was the son of King [Magnus IV of Sweden](/wiki/Magnus_IV_of_Sweden "Magnus IV of Sweden"), Norway and [Scania](/wiki/Scania "Scania"). Margaret succeeded in having her and Haakon's son [Olaf](/wiki/Olaf_II_of_Denmark "Olaf II of Denmark") recognized as heir to the throne of Denmark. In 1376, Olaf inherited the crown of Denmark from his maternal grandfather as King Olaf II, with his mother as guardian; when Haakon VI died in 1380, Olaf also inherited the crown of Norway.[[3]](#cite_note-6)

Margaret became regent of Denmark and Norway when Olaf died in 1387, leaving her without an heir.[[4]](#cite_note-BritannicaMargaret-7) She adopted her great-nephew [Eric of Pomerania](/wiki/Eric_of_Pomerania "Eric of Pomerania") the same year.[[5]](#cite_note-BritannicaErik-8) In 1388, Swedish nobles called upon her help against King [Albert](/wiki/Albert,_King_of_Sweden "Albert, King of Sweden").[[6]](#cite_note-BritannicaSwedenCodeOfLaw-9) After Margaret defeated Albert in 1389, her heir Eric was proclaimed King of Norway.[[4]](#cite_note-BritannicaMargaret-7) Eric was subsequently elected King of Denmark and Sweden in 1396 under the banner of the [House of Griffin](/wiki/House_of_Griffin "House of Griffin").[[4]](#cite_note-BritannicaMargaret-7) His coronation was held in [Kalmar](/wiki/Kalmar "Kalmar") on 17 June 1397.[[7]](#cite_note-BritannicaKalmarUnion-10)

One main impetus for the union's formation was to block German expansion northward into the [Baltic region](/wiki/Baltic_region "Baltic region"). The main reason for its failure to survive was the perpetual struggle between the monarch, who wanted a strong unified state, and the Swedish and Danish nobility, which did not.[[8]](#cite_note-11)

The Union lost territory when [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") and [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland") were [pledged](/wiki/Pledge_(law) "Pledge (law)") by [Christian I](/wiki/Christian_I_of_Denmark "Christian I of Denmark"), in his capacity as King of Norway, as security against the payment of the [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") of his daughter [Margaret](/wiki/Margaret_of_Denmark,_Queen_of_Scotland "Margaret of Denmark, Queen of Scotland"), betrothed to [James III of Scotland](/wiki/James_III_of_Scotland "James III of Scotland") in 1468.[[9]](#cite_note-:1-12) The money was never paid, so in 1472 the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") annexed the islands.[[10]](#cite_note-13)

### Internal conflict

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kalmar_Union&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Internal conflict")]

Diverging interests (especially the [Swedish nobility](/wiki/Swedish_nobility "Swedish nobility")'s dissatisfaction with the dominant role played by Denmark and [Holstein](/wiki/Holstein "Holstein")) gave rise to a conflict that hampered the union in several intervals starting in the 1430s. The [Engelbrekt rebellion](/wiki/Engelbrekt_rebellion "Engelbrekt rebellion"), which started in 1434, led to the overthrow of King Erik (in Denmark and Sweden in 1439, as well as Norway in 1442).[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14) The aristocracy sided with the rebels.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14)

King Erik's foreign policy, in particular his conflict with the Hanseatic League, necessitated greater taxation and complicated exports of iron, which in turn may have precipitated the rebellion.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14) Discontent with the nature of Erik's regime has also been cited as a motivating factor for the rebellion.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14) Erik also lacked a standing army and had limited tax revenues.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14)

The death of [Christopher of Bavaria](/wiki/Christopher_of_Bavaria "Christopher of Bavaria") (who had no heirs) in 1448 ended a period in which the three Scandinavian kingdoms were uninterruptedly united for a lengthy period.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14) [Karl Knutsson Bonde](/wiki/Charles_VIII_of_Sweden "Charles VIII of Sweden") ruled as king of Sweden (1448–1457, 1464–1465, and 1467–1470) and Norway (1449-1450). [Christian of Oldenburg](/wiki/Christian_I_of_Denmark "Christian I of Denmark") was king of Denmark (1448–1481), Norway (1450–1481) and Sweden (1457–1464). Karl and Christian fought over control of Sweden, Norway, and Denmark, leading Christian to seize Sweden from him from 1457 to 1464 before a rebellion led Karl to become king of Sweden again.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14) When Karl died in 1470, Christian tried to become king of Sweden again, but was defeated by [Sten Sture the Elder](/wiki/Sten_Sture_the_Elder "Sten Sture the Elder") in the 1471 [battle of Brunkeberg](/wiki/Battle_of_Brunkeberg "Battle of Brunkeberg") outside Stockholm.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14)

After Karl's death, Sweden was mostly ruled by a series of "protectors of the realm" (*[riksföreståndare](/wiki/Reichsverweser#Sweden "Reichsverweser")*), with the Danish kings attempting to assert control. First of these protectors was Sten Sture, who kept Sweden under his control until 1497, when the Swedish nobility deposed him. A peasant rebellion led Sture to become regent of Sweden again in 1501. After his death, Sweden was ruled by [Svante Nilsson](/wiki/Svante_Nilsson_(regent_of_Sweden) "Svante Nilsson (regent of Sweden)") (1504–1512) and then Svante's son [Sten Sture the Younger](/wiki/Sten_Sture_the_Younger "Sten Sture the Younger") (1512–1520).[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14) Sten Sture the Younger was killed in the 1520 Battle of Bogesund when the Danish king [Christian II](/wiki/Christian_II_of_Denmark "Christian II of Denmark") invaded Sweden with a large army.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14) Subsequently, Christian II was crowned King of Sweden, and supporters of Sten Sture were executed en masse in the [Stockholm Bloodbath](/wiki/Stockholm_Bloodbath "Stockholm Bloodbath").[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14)

#### Swedish War of Liberation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kalmar_Union&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Swedish War of Liberation")]

After the Stockholm Bloodbath, [Gustav Vasa](/wiki/Gustav_I_of_Sweden "Gustav I of Sweden") (whose father, [Erik Johansson](/wiki/Erik_Johansson_Vasa "Erik Johansson Vasa"), was executed) travelled to [Dalarna](/wiki/Dalarna "Dalarna"), where he organized a [rebellion](/wiki/Swedish_War_of_Liberation "Swedish War of Liberation") against Christian II.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14) Vasa made an alliance with Lübeck and successfully conquered most of Sweden.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14) He was elected King of Sweden in 1523, effectively ending the Kalmar Union.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-14) After the [Northern Seven Years' War](/wiki/Northern_Seven_Years%27_War "Northern Seven Years' War"), the [Treaty of Stettin (1570)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Stettin_(1570) "Treaty of Stettin (1570)") saw [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II_of_Denmark "Frederick II of Denmark") renounce all claims to Sweden.[[12]](#cite_note-15)

### End and aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kalmar_Union&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: End and aftermath")]

One of the union's last structures remained until 1536/1537, when the [Danish Privy Council](/wiki/Danish_Privy_Council "Danish Privy Council"), in the aftermath of the [Count's Feud](/wiki/Count%27s_Feud "Count's Feud"), declared Norway a Danish province. In practice, Norway kept its status as a separate kingdom and its own laws, but its council and other central institutions were dissolved, and it became politically subordinate to Denmark.[[13]](#cite_note-16)[[14]](#cite_note-17)[[15]](#cite_note-18) This [Denmark–Norway union](/wiki/Denmark%E2%80%93Norway "Denmark–Norway") lasted nearly three centuries, until Norway was [ceded to Sweden](/wiki/Norway_in_1814 "Norway in 1814") in 1814. The later [Swedish–Norwegian union](/wiki/Union_between_Sweden_and_Norway "Union between Sweden and Norway") lasted until 1905, when Prince [Carl of Denmark](/wiki/Carl_of_Denmark "Carl of Denmark") was elected king of independent Norway.[[16]](#cite_note-19)

According to historian Sverre Bagge, the Kalmar Union was unstable for several reasons:[[9]](#cite_note-:1-12)

* The power of national aristocracies.
* The varied effects of the Kalmar Union's foreign policy on the three kingdoms. For example, attempted expansions into Northern Germany may have served Danish interests, but was costly to Swedes who had to pay higher taxes and were unable to export iron to the Hanseatic League.
* Geography complicated control of the union in the event of rebellion.
* The large territorial size of the union complicated control.
* Denmark was not strong enough to force Norway and Sweden to stay within the union.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kalmar_Union&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"), [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"), and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Kalmarunionen*; [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language"): *Kalmarin unioni*; [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"): *Kalmarsambandið*; [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Unio Calmariensis*
2. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** Norway retained none of its prior possessions, but Christian I pledged the [Northern Isles](/wiki/Northern_Isles "Northern Isles") to [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") as insurance for his daughter's [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") in 1468; the dowry was not paid, and the islands transferred to perpetual Scottish sovereignty in 1470. After the Union's dissolution, all remaining overseas possessions Norway brought into the Union became property of the Danish monarch, who retained ownership following the transfer of the Kingdom of Norway from the Danish crown to Swedish crown (discussed in further detail below) after the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Nominal possession: Norway claimed suzerainty over the island prior to the Union's formation, but it had long since ceased exercising any administrative control over the European settlements there. No direct contact took place between Greenland and the Kalmar Union during the latter's existence.
